label: Customer reply draft (general inbound)
prior_art_examples: Three real products converge on the same shape — agent-assist that drafts, never auto-sends, and that grounds the draft in tenant-specific content. (1) Help Scout AI Drafts: generates a reply trained on the tenant's help center articles, prior conversations, and website copy; agent reviews/edits before send. Help Scout markets this explicitly against autonomous bots: 'agents can review, edit, and personalize drafts before sending… trained on your data to ensure that anything it produces is both accurate and on brand' (helpscout.com/blog/ai-customer-support-software/). (2) Intercom Fin / Copilot: Fin's Copilot mode 'pulls context from past conversations, surfaces instant answers from your knowledge base, and drafts replies' inside the agent inbox; Copilot can also expand a one-line agent note into a full reply or adjust tone (intercom.com Help: 'Let Fin AI Agent answer first'). (3) Chatwoot 'Reply suggestion with AI' (OpenAI integration): produces a candidate reply from conversation history + brand-voice context the operator configures in settings (chatwoot.com/hc/user-guide/articles/1683707349-openai; chatwoot.com/blog/ai-in-chatwoot). Representative output snippets observed in their docs/screenshots follow a four-beat shape: (a) one-line acknowledgement of the customer's specific situation (not 'thanks for reaching out'), (b) the actual answer or next step, (c) any disambiguating question if the ticket is under-specified, (d) sign-off in the founder's voice. Salesforce Prompt Builder formalizes the same idea as 'prompt macros' — reusable templates with {!field} placeholders (admin.salesforce.com/blog/2025/prompt-like-a-pro-ai-prompt-writing-for-salesforce-admins). Zendesk macros are the pre-LLM ancestor: a stored Comment body with {{ticket.requester.name}} placeholders the agent applies manually (support.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/4408844187034). The shift from Zendesk macros to Help Scout/Chatwoot AI Drafts is the shift from 'pick the right template' to 'the AI reads the ticket and assembles the right template'. BBC sits in the Help Scout/Chatwoot camp by default — the founder is the human in the loop, brain memory is the grounding.
prompt_pattern: Few-shot is unnecessary for v1 because the brain itself supplies the examples — voice.example_phrases functions as the one-shot register anchor, and product.positioning/differentiators ground the factual content. The structure that works (matches Help Scout, Chatwoot, Salesforce Prompt Builder convention) is: (1) Role line — 'You are drafting a customer-support reply on behalf of <product.target_user>'s founder.' (2) voiceClause(brain.voice) — register, do_words, dont_words, example_phrases, reused verbatim from the marketing templates' shared helper. (3) Product clause — positioning + 2-3 differentiators in one paragraph so the model doesn't hallucinate the product. (4) Glossary clause — a bulleted list of canonical product terms with their definitions; the model is told 'use exactly these terms, do not rephrase'. (5) Inbound ticket text, fenced. (6) Optional severity hint ('low/medium/high — match urgency in the opening line'). (7) Output instructions — single OutputBlock kind='plain' with markdown body shaped as: short acknowledgement of the specific situation (one sentence, no 'thanks for reaching out' filler), the answer or next step (1-3 short paragraphs), an optional question if disambiguation is needed, founder sign-off. (8) Inline citation instruction (reuse CITATION_INSTRUCTION from templates/types.ts) — every fact-shaping memory gets a <cite mem_id='...'/> tag. Brain injection happens via the same BrainSummary the existing studios already populate, plus a new optional `glossary` field for the support context. The model is told explicitly NOT to wrap the reply in an email-shaped subject/body envelope — the founder is responding inside an existing thread (Help Scout, Intercom, Chatwoot, Front, email reply) and a fresh subject would be wrong.
failure_modes: 1. Overpromising fix dates or specific ETAs ('we'll have this fixed by Friday') — the model has no calendar context and any commitment it invents is a liability. 2. Hallucinating product features that don't exist or are on a roadmap the brain hasn't been told about — mitigated by grounding in product memory + glossary, but still possible on adjacent feature questions. 3. Wrong register for severity: chirpy/upbeat tone on a complaint, or formal/distant tone on a casual question — voice memory is one register; severity should modulate it but in practice the model overshoots cheerfulness. 4. Leaking other customers' info via accidental specificity ('another user with the same setup told us…') — the model has no other-tenant data but can invent plausible-sounding social proof; prompt must forbid 'other customers'/'many users' constructions. 5. Citing memory that doesn't exist — already mitigated repo-wide by stripUnknownCitations() in output-blocks.ts, but worth listing because it's the most visible 'AI looks dumb' failure when the model invents UUIDs. 6. Stilted founder voice — too formal, too many 'apologies for the inconvenience' filler phrases, em-dash-overuse and 'I want to make sure' AI tells; voice.dont_words plus a forbid_pattern override are the lever here.
brain_memory_consumed: voice (REQUIRED, primary) — register sets the tone; do_words/dont_words shape word choice; example_phrases function as the one-shot register anchor (most important single input — without voice memory the studio produces generic Help Scout-flavored prose). product (REQUIRED) — positioning + differentiators ground what the product actually does; without it the model invents features. glossary (REQUIRED — new consumption pattern; today the BrainSummary doesn't surface glossary explicitly but the support studio will need it) — canonical product nouns/verbs the model must use exactly, e.g. 'workspace not org', 'brain not knowledge base', 'studio not generator'. decision (OPTIONAL) — surfaces 'we don't do X' rules that constrain answers (e.g., 'we don't promise on-prem deploys for hobby tier'); cited as <cite mem_id='...'/> when material. team (OPTIONAL) — only used to resolve sign-off; for a solo founder the team table contains one row and the model signs off with that name. vendor (rarely consumed for customer-reply) — only when the inbound is about a known vendor-shaped integration ('Stripe webhook isn't firing'); the vendor memory is then cited so the founder can verify. source_artifact, note, skill — not consumed by this template in v1.
inputs_required: [{id: 'ticket_text', label: 'Inbound message', hint: 'Paste the customer ticket / email / chat message verbatim. Include any prior reply context if the model needs it.', required: true, kind: 'text' (rendered as textarea — needs the templates/types.ts FirstUseInputKind to gain 'textarea' or for the text-kind to render as multiline when hint contains 'paste' — see scoping note). }, {id: 'customer_name', label: 'Customer name (optional)', hint: 'First name is fine; lets the draft open with a real opener instead of 'Hi there'.', required: false, kind: 'text'}, {id: 'severity', label: 'Severity', hint: 'Low = curious question, Medium = blocking but workaroundable, High = production down / paying-customer angry.', required: false, kind: 'select', options: ['low','medium','high'], default: 'low'}, {id: 'context_note', label: 'Anything the AI should know that the customer didn't say (optional)', hint: 'E.g. 'this is a paying customer on the team plan' or 'they emailed 3 days ago and I never replied'.', required: false, kind: 'text'}]. The customer_name + context_note inputs are the 'cheap personalization' levers — Help Scout's product team writes about these being the highest-leverage non-AI inputs in their AI Drafts feature.
output_block_kinds: Use `plain` (markdown body) for v1. The reply lives inside an existing email/chat thread — the founder pastes it into Help Scout, Front, Gmail reply, Intercom, Chatwoot. A new `email_reply` kind with separate {subject, body} fields is tempting but premature: (a) most replies are inside an existing thread where the subject is fixed; (b) when a fresh subject IS needed, the founder writes it in the destination tool; (c) the four typed-preview kinds we already have (x_post, x_thread, threads_post, linkedin_announcement, reel_script, blog_draft) are all 'platform card' shapes, and customer reply doesn't have a single canonical platform shape. If a future v1.x studio (e.g., a 'fresh outbound email' template for Founder Studio) genuinely needs a subject+body card, add an `email_draft` kind then, and reuse it here. v1: kind='plain', props.text is markdown with the body only.
comparable_template_in_existing_studios: Marketing's `single-x-post` is the closest in shape: short, voice-heavy, single OutputBlock of a non-document kind, brain memory injected via voiceClause + product clause, no decisions/vendors clause in the hot path. The difference is that single-x-post produces an outbound platform-shaped post and customer-reply produces an inbound-shaped markdown reply — but the prompt-builder skeleton (role line, voiceClause, product clause, inputs, output instruction) is structurally identical. Engineering's `adr-draft` is wrong as an anchor (it's document-shaped and decisions-heavy); founder's `weekly-recap` is wrong (it's a synthesis over time, not a response to a single artifact).
memory_writeback_proposal: Two flywheel mechanisms, both important. (1) Phrase mining → support/common-replies.md (file-mode) or memory_files rows of kind='note' tagged 'support/common-replies' (DB-mode). When the user accepts a generated reply (or edits and accepts), the studio writes a compact record: {ticket_summary: '<one-line LLM summary of the inbound>', reply_body: '<accepted text>', accepted_at, model, edit_distance_from_draft}. After N=10 accepted records, a background job (or a manual 'consolidate' action in v1, since v1 forbids daemons) clusters them into 'common reply patterns' that get promoted into the glossary or a new memory type `common_reply` — a high-signal voice training input for future drafts. (2) Glossary detection — if the founder edits the draft to swap a noun ('users' → 'members') consistently across 3+ accepted runs, propose a glossary memory addition: kind='glossary', term='members', preferred_over=['users','people']. This surfaces in the queue/ as a regular proposal so Main/Manager can accept. Both writebacks are what makes Support Studio compound; without them it's just an AI-Drafts clone with no improvement loop, which violates ADR-0008's third loop ('Improve').
safety_constraints: Never commit to a specific fix date or ETA. Never promise a refund (founder reviews — model may suggest 'I'll look into a refund' but not 'I've issued a $X refund'). Never use 'we' when the brain.team has only one member — solo-founder voice uses 'I' (forbid_pattern candidate). Never apologize in a way that admits liability ('we're so sorry we failed you' on a bug) — acknowledge the impact without legal admission. Never invent other-customer testimony ('many users tell us…', 'a customer with the same setup…'). Never cite a memory id that isn't in the supplied brain context (already guardrailed by stripUnknownCitations but the prompt should also forbid it). Never use the dont_words from voice memory. Never end with marketing CTAs ('check out our blog!') — this is a support reply, not a newsletter. Never use 'sorry for the inconvenience', 'we appreciate your patience', or other tier-1-support filler unless an override explicitly adds them.
override_examples: 1. 'Always offer a 30-day trial extension if the customer mentions price' → add_constraint with {constraint: 'If the inbound message contains pricing complaints or trial-period concerns, end the reply with a one-line offer of a 30-day trial extension.'}. 2. 'Never say sorry for the inconvenience' → forbid_pattern with {pattern: 'sorry for the inconvenience'}. 3. 'Sign off as 'oc' lowercase, not 'Oscar'' → add_constraint with {constraint: 'Sign off with the single lowercase name 'oc'. Do not use a longer first name or title.'}. 4. 'Open with the customer's first name, never 'Hi there' or 'Hello'' → forbid_pattern + add_constraint pair. 5. 'For paying customers (severity=high), always offer a 15-minute Calendly link' → add_constraint with {constraint: 'When severity is high, include a Calendly link (the team will paste the URL — the draft says "book a 15-minute call here: [calendar link]").'}. 6. 'Use British English spelling' → add_constraint. These mirror what the marketing studio's J.14/J.15 conversational override flow already supports — no new override kinds needed.
estimated_loc: ~140 LOC for the prompt builder (customer-reply.ts) + ~25 LOC of types if a new `glossaryClause` helper goes into the shared types.ts. Total ~165 LOC. Anchored to single-x-post (~120 LOC, simplest marketing template) plus the slightly heavier voice/glossary plumbing this template needs. The new `severity` select input is +5 LOC; the new optional `context_note` input is +5 LOC; the no-new-OutputBlock-kind decision keeps output-blocks.ts and the renderer untouched. If the eventual support/types.ts mirrors eng-templates/types.ts with its own helper file (decisionsClause-equivalent for glossary), add another ~40 LOC one-time which amortizes across all 5 support templates.
v1_essential: Yes. This is the highest-frequency template (5-20x/week for an active solo founder per the outline.yaml description) and it's the one that proves the support studio's voice grounding works. If only one support template ships in v1, this is it. The other four templates (churn-save, incident-status, bug-ack, feature-request-triage) all reuse the same voice + product + glossary plumbing this template establishes, so shipping customer-reply first de-risks the rest.
